sentences.text: Yes.
In Poland, was always anti-Semitism, always.
I cannot say that the majority of people were anti-Semitic.
Some were-- couldn't care.
Most of them couldn't care.
A lot, a big percentage, were anti-Semitic.
In what capacity, in what extent, some were pointing, oh, here is a Jew.
For example, when my father boarded a train-- it was a train to escape-- to escape Warsaw to go to Russia.
Now, I recall.
And even though he had a little bit bigger, big nose, but he didn't look Jewish, with blue eyes.
And he was not recognized.
But one of his formal coworkers, or whatever, pointed, oh, He's a Jew.
Here is a Jew.
And the Polish police [INAUDIBLE] start to beat him.
But he jumped into the wagon, into the, you know the wagon.
And he disappeared from [INAUDIBLE].
And the train moved.
You know, so--
After the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the anti-Semitism was there.
But it was suppressed by the government.
Let me-- let me try to explain certain things.
Jews, especially those who came from Russia, who survived, most of them were trusted for the communist Polish government.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of them left for-- for Israel, for Palestine then, in 1946, 1947-- till 1950, Jews could leave Poland.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Some Jews did not leave.
They stood in Poland.
They were staying in Poland.
In Poland after the war-- after the war, there were, opened, 22 Yiddish schools.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the times of the Six-Day War, we had six schools say-- three schools, I'm sorry-- left in Poland.
Poland, the Polish government, communist government, let the Jews have two newspapers.
Then, it became one newspaper-- two in Yiddish, one in Polish.
The Polish newspaper was even a Zionistic paper.
But it was, later on, liquidated, closed up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were start to be issued books in Yiddish, open schools, as I remember, as I mentioned.
There was two theaters.
Then it was one theater, state-owned theaters.
There was-- there was, and there still is, our Jewish Historical Institute, research.
In Poland, later on, they found, in a ghetto there, in metal jars, the memoirs of Emanuel Ringelblum written in Yiddish.
Yes, please.
OK.
It was maybe not privileged.
But maybe because the Polish Jewry suffered so much, and maybe-- maybe-- maybe it was sort of influential.
I cannot explain.
Because from all these communist countries-- Czechoslovakia, Hungary, Bulgaria-- wherever Jews were and were destroyed, there were no Jewish schools.
Except, in Romania, was a Yiddish theater, I know.
And once in a while, they used to issue a Yiddish book in Yiddish.
Yeah.
But not a-- not a-- including Russia, there was no-no for Yiddish, no-no for Yiddish history, or Jewish history, whatever.
Poland was privileged.
Later on, much later, before we left Poland, I asked one of the leaders of the Jewish leaders, I said, why only Poland?
Why don't we have a-- a cultural life in Czechoslovakia or in Hungary?
He told me, don't ask stupid questions.
That's it.
That's what he told me.
So Poland was privileged to have, you know, the money.
Most of them came from America, from Joint.
The money used to come until 1967-- the Jews of the world, primarily, the United States, to help them-- help them.
And as I said, I grew up-- I grew up in a-- in a Jewish environment, in a Jewish environment, Yiddish environment.
It was not a religious environment.
But it was very, very traditional.
We did not have a synagogue in our city.
It was only a [NON-ENGLISH].
Your father will know what it means.
In Legnica.
But when it comes holidays, Passover was Passover, and Hanukkah was Hanukkah, and Purim was Purim, in school and at home, you know.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anti-Semitism-- in high school, when I went to high school, I was-- when I went to high school, we had-- we had teachers-- it's not a Jewish high school where I was.
There were-- there were-- there were teachers, anti-Semites, anti-Semites.
But it's a different chapter to talk about, you know.
They were.
We felt it.
And I felt it, people, you know.
And especially after the Six-Day War, the anti-Semitism jumped up.
And in 1956, when Gomulka came to power, the anti-Semitism, whenever it was some commotion, the Jews were-- were the-- the point to look at, you know.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
I had a friend, a non-Jew who went to a-- to a dance party, you know, a dance party.
And he had a friend.
And the friend started to talk about Jews.
Even the friend did not know I'm Jewish.
So my friend, my friend, he said, listen, he's Jewish.
Don't talk about Jews around us, something like this.
So he shut up, you know.
A teacher walked in-- it was not with me, but with another Jewish girl in a school, the same school.
He walked in a class.
And he looked around.
He says, finally, this class is clean.
It's pure.
And we knew.
And she stood up, no, not pure.
I'm Jewish here, you know.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] right before we left Poland, in-- in-- after the Six-Day War when I got married, we got already-- our son was born.
And it was in '68-- you know, in '68, when they started anti-Semitic riots in Poland.
This, probably, my wife will be able speak-- to tell you much better than I do.
A woman walked in, are you Jewish?
Yes.
And there's the Jewish child.
This bastard should not be on our soil.
And she threw my boy from-- from the carriage.
My wife was-- was holding him, you know.
Did anything happen to your family about speaking out in communist Poland?
Anything like that?
Yes, my brother.
My brother was sitting in a-- in a-- in a coffee shop with his friends.
There were five friends.
And he was talking political jokes-- jokes, politically jokes, not against Jews, not against Poland, just political jokes.
And one of his friends, not a [INAUDIBLE], a Jew, was probably working for-- for the secret service.
And he probably delivered.
Because, otherwise, how in the world all of these five boys, sort of, were arrested, kept in separate cellars for 72 hours in a-- in a cold, wet cellar-- kept.
They were arrested.
OK.
No.
They were just-- just the-- they came to my mother.
My daddy was not alive anymore.
They came to my mother in '68 when my son-- when my brother was arrested, the youngest brother was arrested.
They came.
They turned the apartment upside down.
They were looking for some evidence of spying, you know.
And finally, they found.
They found an envelope which the cousin-- my father's cousin world to my father in 1949.
A-ha, you have somebody overseas there, in the West.
Do you have any contacts?
And my mother said, no, we don't have-- it's [INAUDIBLE], you know.
So to some of my friends, it happened.
It happened-- arrested, et cetera, et cetera.
But it-- it's special, the anti-Semitism, whenever there was a chance opportunity used to come up, especially after the Six-Day War in '68, '69, when we finally moved from Poland.
How were you able to participate in the Jewish culture, such as it was, in Poland?
How?
Oh, my god.
I have to-- I'm sorry.
I'm speaking to the camera.
I shouldn't jump like this.
I was a teacher, a full-time teacher, of Yiddish, Russian, history of Jews.
I was, first a member, and then a director of two dramatic circles, for a school dramatic circle, dramatic circle theater, and for adult-- for adults.
I took over.
We had a dramatic circle in our Jewish community, always a dramatic circle.
I did receive the first prize, in Poland, for putting together a montage, a potpourri of poetry in Polish.
And I received the first prize for it.
I received the fourth prize, in Poland, for putting on a stage a historical play.
Matter of fact, on the picture it's in front of the castle of the-- of the medieval castle in-- in Legnica.
We're standing-- a play.
And I received the fourth-- fourth prize in Poland.
Of course, I-- I got my director's diploma, certificate.
I received before in 1963.
I was an active writer for the Jewish newspapers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was a journalist, sort of.
Not sort of, but I used to write there for the Jewish newspapers.
I was a camp counselor for Jewish youth camps.
And then I was also-- in some parts, I was an educational director of youth camps.
I used to teach, also, Jewish children Yiddish.
In those cities where they did not have a school or Jewish values, or cultural, or traditions, or customs, or history I used to teach.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
Wow.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In one of the camps, in one of the summer camps, I noticed a 12, 13-year-old girl, beautiful, with long, dark hair to the knees, singing beautifully.
During the-- during the fire camps, she used to sing.
She was singing beautiful, beautiful.
This was the first time I noticed.
But listen, she's a child.
She was a child.
I was-- I was, then, 23 or something, you know, 23.
And, OK.
The other time on the same camp, I had a duty, a duty, you know, to keep order.
After 10 o'clock, after-- kids have to sleep, you know?
I see somewhere a commotion on downstairs.
What's happened?
I see-- I just ran into the downstairs, where the showers used to be for the kids.
And I see a little window from the cellar is open.
And I see some boys running out from the camp.
And among the boys, a girl.
I was waiting.
They returned back after an hour.
I led the boys through.
The girl, I caught.
She was in a sweat suit, sweat suit.
I'm sorry, sorry.
She was in a sweat suit full with apples, here and here, stolen apples in an orchard.
She went-- they went for apples, green apples.
And this was my wife, my future wife.
I told her, where'd you go?
I don't know.
So I picked up her-- her-- you know, the pants.
And all the apples fell down.
And I asked her to stand for two hours as a punishment.
She was standing in front of me.
I was sitting by the table reading a newspaper and eating her apples.
This was my second encounter with her.
And I also make-- used to make pictures, photo pictures, you know, for the kids.
And I used to charge-- charge the cost of the pictures for the kids.
But for her, I gave a special envelope, no charge.
No charge.
And you saw it in an album right now, these pictures I made.
No charge for her.
And that's how I noticed her, beautiful voice, beautiful eyes.
You saw the picture.
Oh, my god, you know.
And it passed by.
It passed by, several years.
Then, my mother-- she was already a widow.
My mother went to-- not far from Warsaw was a rest house for Jews, you know, rest house.
And she met a friend of ours, a mutual friend, my wife's parents' friends and our friend.
And they met and start to talk.
And my wife-- my mother start to say, oh, I have a son.
It's, for him, time to get married.
You know how it is, you know, married.
And he said, he has, also, a daughter.
But I met his daughter.
Not so-- he said, I have a friend in Wloclawek.
They have a daughter, also a teacher.
[NON-ENGLISH], they're saying in Yiddish.
It's something unusual.
So I said, what the heck, she's about 500 kilometers from my place.
So how in the world was I [INAUDIBLE] do?
But I was also, as a teacher, a member of the headquarters of the Jewish organization, you know, the Jewish organization of Poland.
So it was in Warsaw.
Wloclawek was about an-- an hour, hour and a half with a train.
So I said in this particular time, I'm going to visit.
First, we started to write to each other.
Then she sent me two ugly pictures.
I said, I'm not going to meet this girl.
The pictures were terrible, you know.
But I said, what the heck, you know.
I went to Warsaw.
I had the session there.
And after the session, I said-- I called up, from Warsaw to Wloclawek.
They had a phone, which was a luxury in Poland to have a phone.
I called her up.
And I said, may I come?
I'm right now in Warsaw.
May I come visit you in Wloclawek?
And, of course, I spoke to the parents, you know.
And they said, sure, of course.
And I did not know-- it was winter, winter, right before new year's.
And she was horribly sick, my wife.
She was sick.
But she got dressed up.
You know, she dressed up with three or four sweaters on her.
With her father-- by the way, her father was an Auschwitz survivor.
Mengele made experiments on his foot.
This-- this another chapter from her family.
And her mother was a survivor.
But anyway, her father came with her to the railroad station.
I came with flowers, you know.
And I took a look at her.
She was in a fur, warm-dressed.
I said, well, I'm curious how soon I can get a train to-- back to-- to my city.
She got upset, because everybody in a camp, in a school, wherever she used to go, boys were in love with her.
I was the first one who said, I'm ready to go back.
And that's probably what got her.
Wait a minute, I'll-- I'll teach you a lesson.
So finally, OK, we went.
I found out where the train is.
We went to her home.
Her home was, obviously, like warm.
They opened, they-- they-- [INAUDIBLE] by the table.
And they sat down.
They asked me to sit down.
And her father asked me, do you drink?
I said, yes.
OK, let's make a Schnapps, you know, drink.
Meantime, the mother already prepared, and the daughters.
Because beside my wife, there is also a sister and two brothers.
So they were all in the kitchen preparing.
And my-- my wife went to change.
She took off all the sweaters, apparently.
She dressed up and I remember, in a green sweater, very tight, with a skirt, very nicely.
And when I-- and she started to serve Jewish penicillin.
You know it?
[NON-ENGLISH], chicken soup with-- with noodles.
And she put in front of me the first plate, I think.
No, the first was for butter.
Doesn't matter.
And I took a look at her limbs.
I said, oh my god, something-- something nice to look at.
And I said, I'm curious if there is another train.
Together with them was living, also-- also a survivor.
He was alone.
He lived.
And he said, I'll find out.
He went there.
He found out when they have the trains.
Finally, finally, I fell in love with this girl.
And apparently, she fell in love with me.
And April-- it was December and April-- I proposed to her.
I proposed to her in April of '67 in her home town.
Whenever I had the chance, I used to come to her.
And I proposed.
And then the 30th of April, she came my-- to my home, got acquainted her parents with my mother, with my brothers.
And it was the same time it was the unveiling of-- of my-- of the monument of my father's grave.
So it was all together.
And in the 18th of June of '67, a week or 10 days after the Six-Day War, we got married in a very hot summer day.
We-- we wanted to invite a lot of people, a lot of friends.
Some of them were afraid to come.
Some of them could not come.
So, but we got married.
And since then, we are together.
We raised two children.
My-- my son was a year and a half old when we came to the United States.
We came to the United States exactly the same day when the Americans were shot off to the moon, the 16th of July, 1969.
We settled in-- in Atlanta.
And since then, we're living in Atlanta.
I'm curious-- I'm sorry-- I'm curious about your wedding.
Was it a Jewish wedding?
It was a semi-Jewish wedding.
It was not the-- the full Jewish wedding.
But it was-- it had, you know, because of the situation after the Six-Day War in a communist society, it-- it was-- it was very hard.
It was not in a synagogue.
It was in the house.
It was in the house, in her parents' house was the wedding, yes.
No.
But-- but we did, in Beth Jacob-- we belonged to Beth Jacob-- we received our [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], from Rabbi Emanuel Feldman, officially that we are married, you know.
We did it all in the United States.
When we came here, my daughter was born here already, Chana.
My son, Steve, was a year and a half old.
They went to school.
They went to Hebrew academy.
They finished Hebrew academy.
And now, they are adults.
We're waiting for a right [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what I'm talking about, for a right marriage, you know.
And my son is dating somebody.
My daughter is dating, not dating, I don't know right now.
And we're happy with it.
And my daughter works for IBM.
My wife is presently the owner of the International Daily Lilburn.
My son is the manager there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And before that, my wife-- my wife, by the way, was also a teacher of Russian in Poland.
She was the teacher of the year in Legnica.
She was a very good teacher.
And, but when we came to United States, she had to take care of the kids.
So she was in the beginnings, in the beginnings a few years, she was a resident manager of apartments.
So she could take care of manage-- apartments, earn some money, earn the apartment, and take care of the children, look over the children, which was great, which was absolutely great.
And then, she changed her profession.
She went back to school.
She get her master's.
And she became a medical technologist.
She was-- she-- she became-- she was working for, I think, 14 years or 15 years for Georgia Baptist Hospital.
And then, she worked for Northlake Medical Center.
She was the supervisor of the night shift.
But for reasons-- I don't know if it's relevant-- for reasons, she could not stand how the insurance companies are dictating to doctors how long a-- a patient should stay in a hospital.
And that got-- she got frustrated with it.
And she decided to take care of-- the next best thing she can do is to cook.
And she does marvelous.
Well, it's a different story.
Back in Rome, when we-- all the refugees came from Poland when they received-- by the way, we did not receive a-- a passport to leave Poland.
We received a one-way document to leave Poland.
And to receive this one-way document, we had to sign an agreement that we are rejecting the Polish citizenship.
For some of us, it was painful, for some-- because we grew up there.
For some of us, we could not care less.
You understand?
So we received the one-way travel first, to Vienna.
In Vienna, it was Sochnut.
An organization, Sochnut.
Ask her-- they asked us-- to ask us-- I mean, tell us to go to Israel.
Because, officially, all of us were going to Israel.
And they asked me why we decide not to go to Israel, but to the United States.
And I said, in Israel, they do not offer Yiddish.
In Israel, there is no way no-- for-- for a teacher of Russian, same thing, my wife.
And I said-- and this is true-- I'm bound before I left Poland.
I wrote two forwards to the Jewish newspaper, forwards.
If I come to the United States, to New York, if I will be able to work at a Jewish newspaper, they apparently knew me from writing in Polish Yiddish newspapers.
And the-- and the editor or assistant editor named Moishe Crystal-- I remember his name-- wrote me, listen, as long as you are in Poland, we cannot answer you this way or that way.
Come to the United States, we'll talk differently.
And that's what I showed-- showed at the Sochnut, the letter.
They said, no problem.
You'll be-- be transferred to Rome.
In Rome, we were about 5 and 1/2 months.
We found out my wife was pregnant with our daughter.
And the organization, the Jewish organization, the HIAS, tried to find for me a sponsor.
The lady who was our caseworker was a 70-year-old woman from New York.
She knew a little bit Yiddish and a little bit Russian.
So she tried to communicate with me.
She asked me my profession to find a sponsor.
And I told her, I'm a teacher of Russian and also [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means in Russian, Yiddish.
Or, they call it sometimes Yiddish, too.
But I said, [NON-ENGLISH].
In Russian, there is a another word, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means old.
Old, which means, in Russian, [NON-ENGLISH] or 'Hebrew.' So probably, she messed it up, you know.
I don't if she messed it up or not.
She misunderstood me or whatever.
She wrote out.
And she found out that somewhere in this South-- because I asked for South.
I didn't want to take New York when I came to the United States.
I wanted somewhere in the South.
She said, Atlanta needs a teacher for-- needs a teacher like you are.
I said, is it true?
Yes, Atlanta.
Do you know anything about Atlanta?
I said, well, Atlanta, I know, from I know Atlanta from, you know-- it's in the South, it was-- you know [INAUDIBLE] whatever I knew from history, but not too much.
And I was so happy, Atlanta.
And I looked at a map.
It was very close to the ocean.
I wanted by the ocean.
Great.
I said, OK.
Yes, they need a teacher there.
And you, you have a job there.
July the 16, we are landing in Atlanta.
The Jewish family children services, two women are picking us up.
One of them did not speak any-- could not communicate with me.
The other one spoke German.
Since Yiddish and German is a little bit similar, so I could understand her somehow.
And I found out the very first day that, unfortunately, they do not need a Yiddish teacher in Atlanta, they need a Hebrew teacher.
So, sort of, I was stuck in Atlanta.
But I am happy.
I was happy, and I am happy that [INAUDIBLE] Atlanta.
I had different jobs.
I held different jobs, very hard.
I had a very hard beginning.
The first 10 years, very hard.
Very often, I was thinking if I did the right thing to come to the United States.
But this was temporary.
I have to say-- I have to say that some survivors who are living in Atlanta helped us a lot.
I don't know if I should mention names, names of the people who helped us.
[INAUDIBLE] yes.
Borenstein, ?] [?
Garrison, ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [?
Ulman, ?] [?
Juravinski. ?]
Yes.
Yes, survivors.
Lanski. ?] Some of them [?
Erlich. ?] Some of them [?
Hermann. ?] Some of them brought some furniture.
Some brought some food.
Some brought some-- you know, [?
Juravinski ?] put, under the pillow of my son, a $50 bill.
Before my daughter got-- was born, they made a shower.
And the money-- I-- for the money, I could afford to buy a used car, my first used car here.
So if I am speaking about survivors, individual families, individual Jews in Atlanta, they helped us tremendously.
